title: Steady-State Noise Signatures of Lindbladian Exceptional Points
authors: Shihang Pan, Gianmichele Blasi, Géraldine Haack
abstract: Exceptional points (EPs) are non-Hermitian degeneracies at which two or more eigenvalues and their corresponding eigenvectors coalesce. In open quantum systems, exceptional points can arise in the Lindbladian governing the dissipative dynamics. Their signatures have so far been mainly identified in finite-time observables, such as transient currents, while steady-state average currents generally provide no direct evidence of the underlying exceptional-point structure. In this work, we demonstrate that signatures of Lindbladian EPs can nevertheless be accessed in the steady-state regime through current noise. We derive general expressions for current correlation functions within a Lindblad master-equation framework and show, in particular, how exceptional points affect their behaviour as a function of the time delay. We illustrate these results with the paradigmatic example of two interacting qubits coupled to two reservoirs, where the steady-state noise clearly distinguishes overdamped, underdamped, and critical regimes. Our results establish current correlation functions as a steady-state probe of Lindbladian EPs in open quantum systems.
summary: This theoretical work demonstrates that while average currents do not reveal the signatures of Exceptional Points (EPs) in steady-state open quantum systems, current noise correlation functions do. By analyzing these higher-order correlations using a two-qubit model, the authors show that the spectral structure of the noise clearly distinguishes between different dynamical regimes defined by the proximity to an EP.
main_problem: The work aims to find diagnostic signatures of Lindbladian Exceptional Points (LEPs) in the steady-state regime of open quantum systems, as average observables fail to reveal this structure.
main_result: Current noise correlation functions retain clear signatures of LEPs in the steady state, distinguishing them from the behavior of average currents. The coalescence of eigenvalues leads to distinct polynomial time dependencies in the noise spectrum across different dynamical regimes (overdamped, underdamped, critical).
method: The authors derive general expressions for two-point current correlation functions within a Lindblad master equation framework and analyze their spectral properties.
model_or_system: The study uses open quantum systems modeled by the Lindblad master equation, exemplified by two interacting qubits coupled to external reservoirs. The dynamics are analyzed via spectral decomposition of the Liouvillian superoperator into Jordan normal form.
key_observables: Two-point current correlation function (noise), steady-state average currents, and the frequency spectrum of the noise $S^s_{\alpha\alpha'}(\omega)$.
important_parameters: The coupling strength $g$ relative to a critical value $g^*$, which dictates the system's dynamical regime (overdamped, underdamped, or critical).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that local jump operators correctly capture the dynamics and relies on spectral decomposition techniques for solving the master equation.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the distinct temporal structures of steady-state noise functions across overdamped, underdamped, and critical regimes. One figure shows the spectrum of a reduced Liouvillian exhibiting coalescence at an EP.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the theoretical framework using the Lindblad equation, derives expressions for both average currents and current noise, analyzes these observables in the steady state, and finally illustrates the distinct signatures of LEPs by analyzing the two-qubit model across different coupling regimes.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant to AMO physics and open quantum systems as it provides a powerful, non-trivial diagnostic tool (noise spectroscopy) to probe fundamental degeneracies (EPs) in dissipative dynamics where standard measurements fail.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Sketch of a two-qubit system with the following parameters, bare energy of the two qubits ϵ, the inter-qubit coupling strength g, reservoirs characterized by their respective chemical potential and temperature µi, Ti, i = 1, 2 and bare bath-reservoir coupling strengths γ1 and γ2.

Figure 2: Spectrum of the reduced Lindbladian σ  ˜L  on a complex plane showing the coalescence of λ4, λ5 and λ6 at a third-order exceptional point. Color blue represents overdamped regime where η is real while red meas underdamped regime and we change η to iη.

Figure 3: Currents as a function of time. 3a and 3b show the dynamics of I1 (t) and I2 (t) respectively. Both are normalized by their steady-state values. The initial steady is a product of thermal state. We choose the following set of parameters ξ = {ϵ = 1, γ1 = 0.05, γ2 = 0.01, T1 = 1, T2 = 0.5}. And the coupling strength g in each regime are gη2>0 = ¯g/2 and gη2<0 = 5¯g, where ¯g = |γ1 −γ2| /4 = 0.01 represents the value of g at exceptional point. In 3a, two inflexion points occur in both overdamped and ciritical regimes for current 1, while both Ioverdamped 2 and Icritical 2 monotonically grow as 3b shows.

Figure 4: Steady-state noise as a function of the time delay τ. The four panels show the dynamics of Sss 11 (τ), Sss 12 (τ), Sss 21 (τ), and Sss 22 (τ). The delta function δ(τ) is treated as Kronecker delta δτ,0. We choose the following set of parameters ξ = {ϵ = 1, γ1 = 0.05, γ2 = 0.01, T1 = 1, T2 = 0.5}. And the coupling strength g in each regime are goverdamped = ¯g 2 and gunderdamped = 5¯g, where ¯g = |γ1−γ2|

Figure 5 shows the shot noise as a function of g/g∗, where g∗denotes the critical coupling corre- sponding to the exceptional point. As the system is tuned across this point, the shot noise remains a smooth function, with no discontinuities or cusps. Hence, EP does not have a signature in the zero-frequency component of the spectral function of current correlations. However, as demonstrated in the previous section, the analytic properties of the finite-frequency expression provide information about the order of existing EPs.
